text:
2:19 - "And you can make Algaesus." 🤣
2:28 - And that, kids, is how cs188 single-handedly started the "Steamed Algae" meme, way back in 2023.
2:41 - I've been to Akron, I can personally testify to that statement's accuracy.

As someone who helped a friend move to Ohio in 2012, I can confirm it is gross. I actually saw a guy driving his Ford Windstar around town with no windshield in the pouring rain. The guy ended up getting into an accident with the van a couple years ago, it still had no windshield. (Can you imagine the mold in that van? 🤮) Two homeless men decided to, ahem, satisfy each other, in my friend's front yard one night - while my three Goddaughters sat in the living room watching TV after dinner. Whenever a YouTube video pops up about a police chase in the Akron area, I gotta watch just to see if it's my friend, speeding like hell, escaping the state. I even watch Arkansas police chases in case he's made it that far.

It wasn't all bad, though. One of the three girls has ataxia, one has a processing issue and requires extra help, all three dramatically improved their grades within a year. Instead of staff bullying the girl with ataxia (The Connecticut Method) the Akron schools made reasonable accommodations for her issue, and it was discovered that the constant bullying made her symptoms far worse than they really were. The one with processing issues even made Honor Roll because the Special Education staff took the time to find what study methods worked best for her and taught her to use those methods, actually preparing her for Real Life. In Connecticut, she'd take her tests in the Special Education room where the "teachers" would simply give her the correct answers to fill in. 

I miss those girls, but I'm so proud of them and how much better they are performing in a better school system. I cried when the youngest (with the processing issues) sent me a handwritten letter to tell me she had made Honor Roll, she didn't want Mom or Dad to tell me, she wanted to tell Uncle Andy herself, and included the Honor Roll list cut from the newspaper to boot. In three years' time, she had improved dramatically across the board, simply because an educator realized she needed a different method of learning, found that method, and taught it to her. Had she stayed in CT, at least in the school system she was in, she'd have been passed through the system until graduation and left to flounder without any life skills or knowledge, and have little to no chance for independence.

I didn't want to completely smear Ohio, so I figured I'd end it on a positive note.
